video_title: Trend Following Is Changing Faster Than Ever | Systematic Investor | Ep.409
gist: Host Niels Kaastrup-Larsen and systematic investor Yoav Git dissect how equity market microstructure has shifted: a retail-driven explosion of 0DTE options is mechanically suppressing index volatility and pushing intraday autocorrelation negative, which quantifiably erodes short-term trend following while leaving medium/long-term macro trend intact. They frame new products (single-stock futures, compute-power forward curves) and a research agenda around where equity risk now lives (index ~30%, industry/factors ~50%, themes ~5%, idiosyncratic ~45%) and how to build a diversified equity trend program.

themes.theme: New tradable products: single-stock futures & compute

insights.claim: The CME plans to launch single-stock futures on roughly 50 leading US stocks, giving retail and CTAs an easier route to short individual names without borrow/short-sale mechanics.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Watch adoption; easier shorting of expensive AI-era single names could add a new short-side flow and a diversifying risk factor for systematic funds.
insights.quote: the CME has announced that they are going to launch uh 50 odd uh leading US stocks um as a single stock futures on the CME

insights.claim: A firm (referred to as 'Kali'/'culture') is introducing forward curves for the cost of compute power, priced off Nvidia chips, creating a novel commodity-like exposure that has never existed before.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: A tradable compute-cost curve would let investors hedge or speculate on AI infrastructure economics directly rather than via Nvidia equity.
insights.quote: forward curves for uh compute power for the cost of comput power. So that's a like a different uh a different type of uh of commodity which we've never seen before

insights.claim: The tax treatment of single-stock futures (dividend vs price-rise treatment) is unclear versus well-understood index futures, and such practical/tax frictions are what actually stall adoption of new instruments.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Adoption speed of new products hinges on tax clarity, not just design; monitor rulings before assuming liquidity migrates.
insights.quote: it's the the practical things which often are stopping you from getting involved in the market

insights.claim: The main driver of demand for single-stock futures is retail wanting to short an S&P that is widely seen as highly valued at an AI-valuation top, not any impact on CTA strategies.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Positions the product as a bearish retail tool; a rush to short single names could presage a valuation-driven equity pullback narrative.
insights.quote: everybody is kind of calling the the top of the valuation from a valuation perspective, the S&P is very is very highly valued at the moment

insights.claim: Unlike the 2001 telecom fiber that served 20-25 years, AI capex is spent on chips that become obsolete in two to three years, so if AI fails to materialize there is essentially zero recovery value.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Frames AI capex as far more fragile than dot-com infrastructure; a downside scenario implies near-total write-offs, a bear case for chip-heavy capex plays.
insights.quote: all this money which is being thrown at AI is thrown at chips that will, you know, last maybe for 2, three years and then they become old and obsolete

insights.claim: IBM lost about $69 billion of market value in one day (roughly 25%), and fell from an all-time high near 332 on June 2nd to about 213 within roughly six weeks, illustrating how violently single stocks can move.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Single-name tail moves argue for caution before layering leverage via new single-stock instruments; underscores gap risk in concentrated positions.
insights.quote: IBM lost 69 billion of market value in one day and that's about 25% % in one day

insights.claim: The explosion of retail-driven zero-day-to-expiry index options, where retail sells at-the-money straddles to harvest the variance risk premium, concentrates dealer gamma at-the-money near expiry and forces market makers to trade against price moves.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Explains a structural counter-trend flow in equity indices; dealer hedging now dampens moves, key for anyone trading index vol or short-term momentum.
insights.quote: a lot of the retail is about selling at the money straddle... harvesting what's called what's known as the variance risk premier

insights.claim: Because dealers are long concentrated positive gamma from retail 0DTE selling, they sell futures as price rises and buy as it falls, mechanically pulling price back to the strike and acting as a huge daily counter-trend player.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: This dealer-hedging feedback loop is a persistent mean-reversion force in indices; short-term trend strategies now fight it every session.
insights.quote: every day a new counter trend because it's a new zero day to expiry option uh come into the market and is playing anti-trend game in the equity market

insights.claim: The 0DTE hedging loop has turned one-day equity index return autocorrelation negative and suppressed both implied and realized index volatility, keeping the VIX/index vol contained.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Structurally lower index vol and negative autocorrelation is a headwind for index short-term trend and a tailwind for mean-reversion/vol-selling.
insights.quote: the autocorrelation of the intern like in intraday returns or one day returns in in equities have turned negative over time

insights.claim: Implied dispersion (single-stock variance minus index variance) is at an all-time high and equity index correlation at an all-time low, meaning idiosyncratic single-stock risk is elevated while index risk is depressed.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Record dispersion favors dispersion trades and single-stock strategies over index exposure; more harvestable risk now sits in single names.
insights.quote: correlation is at an all-time uh low i.e idiosyncratic risk is at an all-time high

insights.claim: Buying the dip in equity indices, historically a mistake akin to catching a falling knife, has worked reliably over the last decade, with drawdowns like the Trump 'liberation day' selloff recovered within about a month.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: The entrenched buy-the-dip regime is a tradable mean-reversion edge in indices but a structural drag on trend-following signals.
insights.quote: over the last um the over the last 10 years buying the deep actually works much better

insights.claim: The relevant driver of short-term trend decay is volatility-normalized tick size, not raw tick size: as equity indices rose (S&P from ~1000 in 1995 to ~7000) while ticks stayed fixed, the vol-adjusted tick shrank dramatically.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Corrects a common misreading; small vol-adjusted ticks in equities are tied to the microstructure that hurts fast trend, informing which markets fast strategies avoid.
insights.quote: the the volatility normalized tick size and that is very different from just the tick size

insights.claim: A 1% negative one-day autocorrelation costs a pure one-day trend follower about 16 basis points of Sharpe (1% times sqrt of ~252 trading days), but only ~3bps for a 520-style trader with ~20% weight on the short-term signal.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Quantifies the microstructure drag; the effect is modest per market, letting managers size how much fast-trend allocation is worth defending.
insights.quote: that will be uh a 16 basis point sharp negative 16 basis point sharp on your on your uh on your performance

insights.claim: The roughly 3% cumulative drop in equity autocorrelation over 20 years scales to about 20 basis points of Sharpe of damage for short-term equity-portfolio traders after accounting for limited diversification.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Explains why the industry has drifted slower; 20bps of Sharpe erosion is meaningful for fast programs but not catastrophic across a diversified book.
insights.quote: we've seen maybe a drop of 3% in autocorrelation in the equity market over over the last 20 years

insights.claim: Trend returns are driven mainly by macroeconomic and secular trends rather than order-book structure, so slower long-term trend followers largely avoid the counter-trend retail game and harvest a different alpha source.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Supports allocating to medium/long-term trend over fast strategies in the current regime; the alpha source is more durable.
insights.quote: trends are driven really by macroeconomic events more than just the order book structure

insights.claim: Referencing Toby Crabel, markets that opened higher used to close higher about 58% of the time in the past but now only ~53-54%, materially reducing intraday follow-through for short-term managers.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Independent corroboration that intraday momentum has weakened; a headwind stacked on execution costs for fast strategies.
insights.quote: in the old days markets that open higher they would generally also move higher and close higher during the day something like 58% of the time. Now... we're down to 54 53%

insights.claim: Trading the front commodity contract captures roughly 80% of the relevant risk and 90-99% in financials but less in commodities, so capturing distinct risk factors requires deliberately trading further out the curve.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Sets expectations for how much diversification back-curve trading actually adds; guides where to spend liquidity budget for real risk diversification.
insights.quote: let's capture 80% of the risk with just trading the front contract

insights.claim: Front commodity contracts are dominated by CTAs, market makers and retail trading demand-driven dynamics, while contracts several years out are dominated by physical producers/consumers hedging long-term supply, cost of production and new-crop uncertainty.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Different participants mean different, potentially diversifying risk factors down the curve; back-month exposure is a distinct trade, not a leveraged front-month.
insights.quote: if you go out you know 3 years out into the oil curve uh it is mainly dominated by physical players who really want to hedge their production

insights.claim: The industry generates trend signals from the continuous front-month history rather than the individual tradable contract because near expiry a contract's volatility converges to the front, making the prior front-month history the more relevant object.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Explains a core systematic-design choice; both approaches are valid, but seasonal markets like natural gas warrant per-contract vol adjustment at rolls.
insights.quote: most of the industry is looking at essentially the history of the first contract

insights.claim: Carry and roll-yield strategies (unlike trend) must account for commodity seasonality, since computing carry between, for example, June and December natural gas contracts is meaningless because the two are effectively unconnected.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Flags a key pitfall for carry strategies in seasonal commodities; naive carry calculations across seasons produce garbage signals.
insights.quote: if you're trying to calculate a carry in natural gas between the June and the December contract good luck to you

themes.theme: Where equity risk lives: factors, themes, narratives

insights.claim: The equity index now explains only about 25-30% of single-stock variance (down from ~35%), adding industry and Barra-style factors lifts explained risk to ~50%, thematic exposure adds ~5%, leaving ~45% genuinely idiosyncratic.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: A risk-decomposition map for equities; shows how little incremental risk themes add and where diversification budget should go.
insights.quote: the index might have explained 35%. These days it might be explaining 30 or 25%

insights.claim: Themes are transient, news-driven risk factors (World Cup, Strait of Hormuz, COVID, semiconductors) distinct from stable industry or Barra factors, and semiconductors can act as both an industry and a theme simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Distinguishing themes from industries prevents double-counting exposure when building thematic or trend baskets.
insights.quote: themes are like risk factors but the things that are transient and they are driven by news

insights.claim: Many thematic ETFs and bank QIS baskets fail to capture orthogonal theme volatility because they are over-exposed to underlying industry, country, currency or Barra factors, so investors seeking pure theme exposure are better off constructing baskets themselves.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Buyers of thematic ETFs/QIS may be paying for industry beta, not the theme; a case for bespoke basket construction.
insights.quote: a lot of ETFs are actually too much exposed to basically to the industry... and are actually not representing

insights.claim: The 'thematic investing as missing factors' paper by Wei Li reframes thematic investing as a risk-identification problem rather than a prediction problem, prioritizing knowing where risk comes from before attempting to forecast.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Signals an academic shift toward risk decomposition; useful lens for portfolio construction before signal design.
insights.quote: it's it's it's less about prediction. It's really about risk about trying to identify where is the risk coming from

insights.claim: Ronnie Sadka's 'narrative momentum' paper builds a point-in-time dataset of 13.3 million news articles over about 11-12 years and shows investors underreact to slow-evolving themes over roughly six-month horizons, making news trend-followable.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Documents a tradable underreaction-to-news effect; a potential new alpha source overlapping little with price momentum.
insights.quote: a really unique data set that they've done that curated uh news and like millions of articles

insights.claim: Estimating a stock's beta to a narrative is mathematically fragile because narratives drive only ~5% of volatility, producing large estimation error, which is why a human overlay on thematic baskets (e.g. recognizing Coca-Cola benefits from the World Cup) can outperform pure quant construction.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Warns that quant narrative baskets carry high estimation error; discretionary overlays may add value where signal-to-noise is low.
insights.quote: only about 5% of the volatility is driven by the narrative... that makes the estimation error on like being able to construct a genuine basket difficult

insights.claim: The narrative-underreaction edge documented for 2012-2023 is likely being arbitraged away rapidly as AI/machine-learning now instantly digests all news, turning it into a largely solved problem over the last five years.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: Caution against deploying capital into narrative-momentum strategies whose alpha may already be decaying as AI compresses reaction time.
insights.quote: that underreaction overreaction is potentially going to be away quite quickly

insights.claim: With agentic AI, news is increasingly generated individually per user by social/algorithmic feeds, so the 'narrative truth' becomes personal and injects individual bias into trading strategies built on news.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Second-order risk: news-based systematic signals may fragment as information itself personalizes, undermining shared-narrative assumptions.
insights.quote: with Agentic AI as it comes uh you know news is actually not anymore being fetched from some kind of database it's actually being generated for each of us individually

themes.theme: Building diversified equity trend & current tape

insights.claim: A diversified equity trend program layers index futures (~30% of equity risk), easily-traded industry/sector ETFs and futures, then Barra-factor and single-stock long/short construction, yielding a trend book almost uncorrelated to the standard index-trend component.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: A blueprint for adding a diversifying, near-uncorrelated equity-trend sleeve; each layer must avoid double-counting overlapping exposures.
insights.quote: you can actually create um sort of a CTA a trend program in equities um which is like almost uncorrelated to the first component

insights.claim: July 2026 has been broadly counter-trend across fixed income, FX and commodities, with idiosyncratic risk in Japanese bonds, oil whipsawing on the lapsed ceasefire, and tech and Japanese equities selling off.
insights.speaker: Yoav Git
insights.so_what: A counter-trend month pressures trend managers; oil dispersion depends on speed, with slow traders staying long and fast traders caught short.
insights.quote: July has been counter trend um in general both you know in fixed income certainly and then FX

insights.claim: As of mid-July, trend benchmarks are roughly flat-to-slightly-down on the month but positive YTD: the BTOP50 up ~8.5%, SG CTA index up ~9%, SG Trend up ~8.76%, and the SG Short-Term Traders index the weakest, down ~1.12% in July and only ~4% YTD.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Short-term traders lagging both monthly and YTD corroborates the thesis that fast strategies face structural headwinds versus classic trend.
insights.quote: the short-term traders index... that's hurting a little bit more down 1.12% in July um uh still up 4% for the year

insights.claim: Sins Capital's Macro Terminal ranked hedge fund strategies by blow-up/forced-liquidation risk, placing short-vol/short-gamma as highest risk ('nuclear') and CTA managed futures near the safest at 15th of 17, with vol-targeted diversified macro second-lowest.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Reinforces the case for risk-managed CTAs as defensive 'second responders' versus short-vol strategies with unlimited-downside tail risk.
insights.quote: the worst one, the nuclear one uh was shortv tail premier short gamma... CTA Minus futures came in as number 15 out of 17
